text: Pretty sure that won't qualify as a true ferrofluid, as a true ferrofluid has some curious properties like the iron not being able to be pulled out of it by a strong magnetic field or similar. hi rob,
i was going very well and got my 1/3 and 2/3 mix and started adding 50% h202 until my 2/3 mix stopped reacting. i then mixed my iron 3 with my iron 2 and then in the left over in the bottle added 20% ammonia and i could see black bits form. feeling confident i went ahead and added some ammonia to my iron mix and to my dismay it went pee yellow and no black precipitate.. what on earth did i get wrong.and what do you think i actual have now? Regards andy

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Do you know - that is one of the things I was thinking about when i began this particular series of experiments. I have yet to try it - but I did come across an interesting paper that discussed using cement as a piezo material. Basically you add salt to the cement and let it dry between two copper plates with a 5v potential between them. After 24hours you have a piezo active material - apparently - again I haven't tried it - but it did look fascinating.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It is conductive - just not very conductive. Having said that - it is a fascinating material that deserves more attention than we are giving it. I am really glad you pointed me in this direction it has spurred a whole new set of ideas and explorations.

author: @manwar999
text: emfutur.com/43-iron-oxide

author: @manwar999
text: Fe3O4 is ferrimagnetic with a Curie temperature of 858 K. There is a phase transition at 120K, the so-called Verwey transition where there is a discontinuity in the structure, conductivity and magnetic properties. This effect has been extensively investigated and various explanations have been proposed. Fe3O4 is an electrical conductor with significantly higher (X 106) conductivity than Fe2O3, and this is ascribed to electron exchange between the FeII and FeIII centres.

author: @manwar999
text: I’m confused by the fact the fe3o4 conductivity was very poor when my research showed that fe3o4 was a conductor...now I’m finding controversy as to whether it is or isn’t conductive...now more it is not than is a conductor, and for this I am sorry...here in a repost is what I originally based my findings on...will be more careful in the future…

text:
you must wear safety glasses when operating the centrifuge…one stress crack in the flywheel and the item your centrifuging becomes a missle...
